persona.name: Sofia Gutierrez

occupation.title: Third-Grade Teacher
occupation.organization: Alamo Heights Elementary School, San Antonio ISD
occupation.description: Teaches reading, math, and science to 8-year-olds. Spends her days managing a classroom of 22 kids, grading papers, and creating lesson plans on a modest salary. The job is rewarding but exhausting, and her budget is tight.

persona.education: B.S. in Elementary Education from UTSA, 2022. Dean's List. Passionate about literacy and bilingual education. Student loans still being paid off.
long_term_goals: Pay off her student loans within 5 years
Get a master's in reading specialist certification
Prove that drugstore skincare can outperform prestige brands
Eventually buy a small house in her neighborhood
persona.style: Sofia is warm, organized, and practical in a way that comes from managing 22 third-graders all day. She speaks clearly with a slight Tex-Mex inflection and a teacher's habit of over-explaining things patiently. She dresses in colorful but washable teacher outfits — she's been sneezed on too many times for silk. Her beauty approach is strategic: she knows exactly what's in every product on her shelf and how much it costs per ounce. She's the friend who sends you the dupe before you buy the expensive version.

traits: Resourceful and budget-conscious without being cheap
Nurturing and loves helping others find the right products
Proudly contrarian about prestige skincare — enjoys proving dupes work
Organized to a fault — color-codes her product rotation
Patient and warm but firm about her opinions
Finds genuine joy in saving money without sacrificing quality

interests: Finding drugstore dupes for prestige skincare products
Budget beauty YouTube (Hyram, Dr. Dray archives)
Target shopping — especially the beauty aisle
Volunteering at her church's youth programs
likes: CeraVe, Vanicream, The Ordinary, Neutrogena Hydro Boost
Products under $15 that deliver real results
Detailed ingredient lists she can compare across brands
Sharing her drugstore finds with friends and coworkers
When a $9 product outperforms a $65 one
Sunday evening skincare prep for the week
dislikes: The implication that expensive products are inherently better
Brands that charge extra for 'clean' or 'natural' marketing
Influencers who only recommend products they got for free
Feeling like skincare is only for people with more money
Coworkers who judge her for using 'cheap' products

beliefs: Great skincare should be accessible to everyone, regardless of income
Education is the most important job in the world, even if the pay doesn't reflect it
Smart shopping is a skill, not a compromise
Community matters — share what you know to help others
skills: Building effective routines on a strict budget
Classroom management (translates to patience in all areas)

general: Compares price-per-ounce before buying any skincare product
Keeps a running list of products she wants to try when they go on sale
Uses every product to the last drop — cuts tubes open to get the remaining product
Posts drugstore dupe recommendations on her private Instagram for friends

morning: The Ordinary hyaluronic acid, CeraVe moisturizing cream, Neutrogena sunscreen
Light concealer and brow pencil — minimal makeup for school
Breakfast tacos she made the night before, coffee in a thermos
workday: Teaches from 7:45 to 3:15 — on her feet all day
Lunch in the teacher's lounge — always meal-prepped
After school: grading and lesson planning until 4:30
Picks up her younger sister from middle school
evening: Gentle cleanser and her nighttime rotation
Alternates: retinol (The Ordinary) Mon/Wed, glycolic acid Fri
Cooks dinner for herself and her sister
Watches budget beauty YouTube while grading papers
weekend: Target run — checks the skincare clearance section first
Church on Sunday morning with her family
Meal prep for the whole week
Visits her abuela and catches up with extended family

health.physical: Healthy but tired. On her feet all day, which causes sore legs by evening. Occasional stress breakouts during report card season. Mild combination skin — oily T-zone, dry cheeks in winter. Takes vitamin D supplements on her doctor's recommendation.
health.mental: Resilient but carries the emotional load of teaching. Cares deeply about her students, which sometimes means she takes work stress home. Her skincare routine is one of her few indulgences and a genuine self-care moment.

relationships.name: Rosa Gutierrez
relationships.description: Works housekeeping at a hotel. Raised Sofia and her sister on a tight budget. Sofia's thriftiness comes directly from watching her mom stretch every dollar. Uses Pond's cold cream — has for 30 years.

relationships.name: Isabella Gutierrez
relationships.description: High school sophomore who Sofia is helping raise. Isabella is getting into skincare and Sofia is teaching her a budget-friendly routine. Their bonding time often involves Target beauty aisle browsing.

relationships.name: Abuela Lupe
relationships.description: Lives nearby. Uses aloe vera from her garden and coconut oil for everything. Sofia respects her grandmother's approach but also insists on sunscreen.

relationships.name: Melissa Tran
relationships.description: Third-grade teacher in the classroom next door. They share lesson plans, lunch duty complaints, and skincare recommendations. Melissa got Sofia into The Ordinary.
